topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
Discrete Mathematics > Graph Theory > Matchings, Marriage Lemma, Tutte's theorem
prompt: At a round table are seated $n$ boys and $n$ girls, where $n > 3$. In every move, it is allowed to swap the sitting places of two adjacent children. The entropy of a sitting arrangement is the minimum number of moves resulting with each child having at least one neighbor of the same gender. Find the maximum possible entropy of a sitting arrangement.
response_text:
Let us encode boys by \(B\) and girls by \(G\). We have a cyclic word of length \(2n\) with \(n\) \(B\)'s and \(n\) \(G\)'s.

We want the minimum number of adjacent swaps needed to reach a configuration in which **every child has at least one neighbor of the same gender**. Then we seek the maximum possible value of this minimum over all initial seatings.

## 1. What do the “good” final arrangements look like?

If every child has at least one neighbor of the same gender, then there can be no isolated \(B\) or isolated \(G\). So, around the circle, each maximal block of equal letters has length at least \(2\).

Thus a good arrangement is a cyclic concatenation of blocks
\[
B^{a_1}G^{b_1}B^{a_2}G^{b_2}\cdots
\]
with all \(a_i,b_i\ge 2\).

In particular, the alternating arrangement
\[
BGBG\cdots BG
\]
is the “worst-shaped” one, since every child has two neighbors of the opposite gender.

We will show that this alternating arrangement has entropy \(\left\lceil \frac n2\right\rceil\), and no arrangement can have larger entropy.

---

## 2. Upper bound for every arrangement

Consider any arrangement. Let \(t\) be the number of gender changes as we go around the circle, i.e. the number of adjacent pairs \(BG\) or \(GB\). Since the circle is balanced, \(t\) is even, and \(2\le t\le 2n\).

A good arrangement has every monochromatic block of length at least \(2\), so if there are \(t\) changes, then there are \(t\) blocks, each of size at least \(2\). Hence in a good arrangement,
\[
2t\le 2n \quad\Rightarrow\quad t\le n.
\]
So we need to reduce the number of changes to at most \(n\).

Now one adjacent swap changes the number \(t\) by at most \(2\). Therefore, if initially \(t=t_0\), then at most
\[
\frac{t_0-n}{2}
\]
swaps are needed in the best case to bring \(t\) down to \(n\). Since \(t_0\le 2n\), this gives
\[
\text{entropy} \le \frac{2n-n}{2}=\frac n2.
\]
Thus for every arrangement,
\[
\text{entropy}\le \left\lceil \frac n2\right\rceil.
\]

So the maximum possible entropy is at most \(\left\lceil \frac n2\right\rceil\).

---

## 3. Alternating arrangement needs \(\left\lceil \frac n2\right\rceil\) moves

Now start from the alternating circle
\[
BGBG\cdots BG.
\]

In any good final arrangement, every block has length at least \(2\). Since there are \(n\) boys and \(n\) girls, there are at most \(\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) boy-blocks and at most \(\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) girl-blocks.

Equivalently, compared to the alternating arrangement (which has \(n\) boy-blocks and \(n\) girl-blocks, all of length \(1\)), we must merge many singleton blocks. Each adjacent swap can decrease the number of monochromatic blocks of one gender by at most \(1\), hence can decrease the total number of blocks by at most \(2\). Starting from \(2n\) blocks and ending with at most \(n\) blocks, we need at least
\[
\left\lceil \frac{2n-n}{2}\right\rceil=\left\lceil \frac n2\right\rceil
\]
swaps.

It remains to show this many swaps suffice.

### Construction
- If \(n=2m\) is even, from
  \[
  BGBG\cdots BG
  \]
  swap positions \((2,3), (6,7), (10,11),\dots,(4m-2,4m-1)\), i.e. every other \(GB\).  
  After \(m\) swaps the arrangement becomes
  \[
  BBGGBBGG\cdots BBGG,
  \]
  which is good. So entropy \(\le m=\frac n2\).

- If \(n=2m+1\) is odd, do the same for \(m\) disjoint places, yielding
  \[
  BBGGBB\cdots G B G
  \]
  with one alternating \(B,G,B,G\)-segment of length \(2\) left around the wrap; one extra adjacent swap fixes this, for a total of \(m+1=\frac{n+1}{2}\) swaps.

So the alternating arrangement has entropy exactly
\[
\left\lceil \frac n2\right\rceil.
\]

Since no arrangement can have larger entropy, this is the maximum possible entropy.

Final answer: \(\left\lceil \frac n2 \right\rceil\)
